concept_name: Variation of g with Height and Depth
locked_facts: At height h: g_h = g(1 - 2h/R) for h << R; exact: g_h = GM/(R+h)²
At depth d: g_d = g(1 - d/R) — g decreases linearly with depth
g is maximum at the surface and decreases both above and below
Above surface: g ∝ 1/(R+h)² — inverse square decrease
Below surface: g ∝ (R-d) — linear decrease (only mass below contributes)
Shell theorem: a uniform shell exerts zero gravitational force on a mass inside it
truth_statements: The acceleration due to gravity decreases with height above the surface following an inverse-square law
Below the surface g decreases linearly with depth becoming zero at the centre of the Earth
The variation of g with depth is linear because only the mass below the point contributes — the shell above exerts zero net force
At the surface of Earth g ≈ 9.8 m/s² and this is the maximum value for a uniform Earth
The approximation g_h = g(1 - 2h/R) is valid only when h is much smaller than R

aha_moment.what_to_show: A graph of g vs distance from Earth's centre: linear increase from centre to surface (g ∝ r), then 1/r² decrease outside. The surface is the peak. An animation shows a mass falling through a hypothetical tunnel — it accelerates until the surface, then decelerates, performing SHM.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Combined g vs r graph with linear rise inside and 1/r² fall outside, peaking at R
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students assume g is constant everywhere or increases toward the centre — the graph showing zero at the centre with linear rise is the key insight

simulation_states.label: g at height
simulation_states.description: Object moved to height h above surface. g_h = GM/(R+h)² shown. As h increases, g decreases rapidly. At h = R, g drops to g/4.
simulation_states.emphasis: g decreases as 1/(R+h)² above the surface — not linearly

simulation_states.label: g at depth
simulation_states.description: Object moved below the surface. At depth d, only mass in sphere of radius (R-d) contributes. g_d = g(1-d/R). At centre: g = 0. Shell above contributes nothing.
simulation_states.emphasis: Only mass BELOW you pulls you down — the shell above cancels out

simulation_states.label: Complete g vs r graph
simulation_states.description: Full graph from r = 0 to r >> R. Inside Earth: linear rise (g ∝ r). At surface: maximum. Outside: 1/r² decrease. The surface is the peak.
simulation_states.emphasis: g is maximum at the surface — it decreases both above AND below

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student moves a probe from centre through surface to space. g value and direction shown. Graph builds in real time. Tunnel-through-Earth demo shows SHM of falling object.
simulation_states.emphasis: Drag from centre to space — watch g rise then fall. Surface is the maximum.

common_misconceptions.belief: g increases as you go toward the centre of the Earth
common_misconceptions.correction: g decreases linearly as you go deeper, reaching zero at the centre. The shell of mass above you exerts zero net force (shell theorem). Only the mass below you contributes.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate object sinking into Earth with g value decreasing to zero at centre

common_misconceptions.belief: g decreases linearly with height above the surface
common_misconceptions.correction: Above the surface, g follows an inverse-square law: g = GM/(R+h)². The linear approximation g(1-2h/R) only works for h << R. At large heights, the actual decrease is much slower than linear.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Plot linear approximation vs exact 1/r² — they diverge rapidly at large h
